required.title: [Read Aloud] High-quality picture books and early chapter books
required.author: Jane Yolen (sensory detail), *The Snowy Day* by Ezra Jack Keats (simple narrative), *Charlotte's Web* by E.B. White (beautiful prose at every level), *Where the Wild Things Are* by Maurice Sendak (story structure in its purest form).

suggested.title: A copywork book or blank journal for copywork

competence_markers.description: The student writes or dictates in their Writer's Notebook daily, producing at minimum a sentence and a drawing
The student can compose a thought orally — holding it in mind and delivering it as a clear, complete sentence — for transcription
The student understands that writing is captured thought and can explain in their own words why people write things down
The student can copy a short passage by hand with attention to the words and punctuation
The student can draw a scene and write at least two sentences describing what is happening in it
The student understands the concept of a "draft" — that first attempts are starting points, not finished products
The student has practiced at least one revision: reading their own work aloud, identifying something to change, and producing an improved version
The student shows willingness to write without excessive anxiety about spelling or neatness — fluency is developing ahead of correctness

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me something you wrote in your notebook this week.
narration_prompts.detail: Read it to me. What made you decide to write about that?
When you write something for the first time, is it finished? What can you do with it after?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you changed something you wrote.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you change? Why? Did it get better?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is your favorite book that we read together?
narration_prompts.detail: Pick a sentence from it that you really like. Why do you like how it sounds?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me a story about something that happened to you.
narration_prompts.detail: Now — can you write that story down? What is the first sentence?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why do people write things down instead of just saying them?
narration_prompts.detail: What happens to words that are written? What happens to words that are only spoken?

cross_connections.description: The mechanics of letter formation, spelling, and reading develop alongside writing. The student who is learning phonics in 3.1.1 is building the same sound-symbol connections needed to move from dictation to independent writing. These two packets reinforce each other daily.

The narrations that assess history work are writing practice. The student who narrates what they learned about Mesopotamia or Egypt is practicing oral composition and early written expression. History gives writing content; writing gives history a voice.
The daily notebook habit is an IC1.1 discipline. The student who has learned to follow routines and maintain daily practices in IC1.1 can sustain the daily writing habit that 3.2.1-A1 requires.

As the student moves from notebook entries to short stories, the narrative skills developed in 3.2.3-A (character, problem, resolution) give shape to their daily writing. The writing process and story structure develop in parallel.
The willingness to revise — to look at what you made and make it better — is the same craftsmanship standard introduced in IC2.1. A writer who revises is a craftsperson who cares about quality.

The student who has built something with their hands understands drafts intuitively. The first attempt at a shelter was not perfect either. You looked at it, saw what was wrong, and fixed it. Writing works the same way.

content.why_this_matters:
You are going to learn how to make your thoughts visible. Right now, when you have an idea or a story or something you want to say, it lives inside your head. Nobody else can see it. It stays there until you forget it — and you will forget it, because that is what happens to thoughts that are not captured. Writing is how you capture them.

This is not about handwriting practice or spelling tests. This is about something much bigger: learning that the words in your mind can become words on a page, and that once they are on the page, other people can read them, think about them, and be changed by them. A letter you write can make someone happy. A story you tell can make someone laugh or cry. An idea you put down on paper can last longer than you can — books written thousands of years ago are still being read today. That is the power you are learning to use.

You do not need to have perfect handwriting to begin. You do not need to spell every word correctly. You do not even need to hold a pencil yet — you can speak your thoughts and someone else can write them down for you, and those words are still yours. What matters is that you begin. Every writer who ever lived started exactly where you are: with something to say and a willingness to say it.

The habits you build now — putting thoughts into words every day, telling stories, describing what you see, keeping a notebook — are the same habits used by every great writer in history. You are not preparing to be a writer someday. You are a writer now. The only question is whether you practice.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The connection between thought and written word** — the understanding that writing is captured thought, and that any thought you can speak, you can write
2. **Dictation as a legitimate writing method** — for students who cannot yet write independently, dictation builds the writer's identity before the mechanics arrive
3. **The habit of daily writing** — the writer's notebook as a personal, unjudged space for words and ideas
4. **Drawing as a bridge to writing** — using illustration to scaffold and extend written expression
5. **The concept of a draft** — the idea that first attempts are starting points, not finished products
6. **Oral composition skills** — the ability to compose a thought, hold it in mind, and deliver it as a coherent statement
7. **Copying as apprenticeship** — the practice of copying good writing by hand to absorb rhythm and structure
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Your Words Matter

Every person has things to say. You notice things other people miss. You remember things that happened to you. You imagine things that have not happened yet. You have opinions about what is fair and what is not, what is beautiful and what is boring, what is scary and what is wonderful.

All of those thoughts — every single one — can be turned into writing. And writing does something that talking cannot: it stays. When you tell someone a story, the story lives in their memory, and memories fade. When you write the story down, it lives on the page. Someone can read it tomorrow, or next year, or a hundred years from now.

You might think that writing is only for grown-ups, or only for people who have learned all the rules. That is not true. Writing is for anyone who has something to say. The rules come later. First comes the saying.

### Part 2: Dictation — Speaking Your Writing

If you cannot write all your letters yet, or if writing by hand is slow and frustrating, you can still be a writer. Here is how: you speak, and someone writes down what you say. This is called dictation, and it is one of the oldest ways of writing in the world. Kings dictated letters to scribes. Generals dictated orders. Milton, one of the greatest poets in the English language, dictated his masterpiece *Paradise Lost* because he was blind.

When you dictate, here is what happens:
1. You think about what you want to say
2. You say it clearly, one sentence at a time
3. An adult writes your words down exactly as you said them
4. You see your spoken words become written words on the page
5. The adult reads your words back to you

That last step is important. When you hear your own words read back, you will sometimes think, "That's not quite what I meant," or "I want to add something." That is the beginning of revision — changing your writing to make it say what you really mean. You are already doing what every writer does.

As your handwriting develops, you will gradually take over the writing yourself. But even then, dictation remains a useful tool. Many professional writers dictate their first drafts because ideas flow faster through speech than through the hand.

### Part 3: The Writer's Notebook

You are going to keep a notebook. It is yours. No one grades it. No one corrects the spelling. No one tells you what to write in it. It is the place where you put your words — whatever words you want.

**What goes in the notebook:**
- What you saw today that was interesting
- A story about something that happened to you
- A description of a person, animal, or place
- A list of things you love, or things that scare you, or things you wonder about
- A picture with words underneath
- A word you heard that you liked the sound of
- A question you want to answer
- Anything. The only rule is that you put something in it every day.

The notebook is where you practice being a writer. Just as a musician practices scales every day — not because scales are music, but because they build the skills that make music possible — you write in your notebook every day because daily writing builds the skills that make real writing possible. Some days it will be easy. Some days you will stare at the blank page and think you have nothing to say. Write anyway. Write "I have nothing to say today" and then keep going, because the act of writing pulls thoughts out of places in your mind you did not know were there.

### Part 4: Drawing and Writing Together

For the youngest writers, drawing is not a substitute for writing — it is a partner. When you draw a picture, you are thinking about what to write. The picture shows you what the scene looks like. Then you write what is happening in the scene.

Here is how it works:
1. Draw a picture of something — a real event, an imagined scene, a person, an animal
2. Look at your picture carefully
3. Write at least one sentence about what is happening in the picture
4. As you get more comfortable, write more sentences: what happened before? what happened after? what does it sound like? what does it smell like?

The drawing helps you think. The writing helps you say what you think. Together, they are more powerful than either one alone.

### Part 5: Writing What You Hear — Copying Good Writing

One of the best ways to learn to write is to copy writing that is already good. This is not cheating. It is how writers have learned for centuries. Benjamin Franklin taught himself to write by copying essays he admired and then trying to write them from memory. Hunter S. Thompson typed out *The Great Gatsby* word by word just to feel what great sentences felt like under his fingers.

Here is the practice: your mentor chooses a short passage from a book you love — two or three sentences. You copy it by hand, word by word, paying attention to every letter, every period, every comma. You are not just practicing handwriting. You are absorbing the rhythm of good prose. You are learning how sentences are built by building them yourself.

Do this several times a week. Over months, you will start to notice patterns in how good writers write. You will start to hear a rhythm in your own writing that was not there before. The practice is simple, and its effects are deep.

### Part 6: Telling Stories Aloud — Oral Composition

Before you write a story, tell it. Sit with your mentor and say, "Let me tell you about the time..." or "Once there was a..." and just tell the story. Let it come out of your mouth before it goes onto the page.

This is called oral composition, and it matters because speaking is easier than writing. When you speak, you do not have to worry about spelling or handwriting or punctuation. You can focus on the story itself — what happened, who was there, how it felt.

After you tell the story, then write it. You will find that the writing comes easier because you have already found the words once. You know where the story goes. You know the important parts. Now you are just putting them down on paper.

Practice this: every day, before you write in your notebook, tell someone what you are going to write about. Even thirty seconds of talking makes the writing smoother.

### Part 7: What Is a Draft?

Here is one of the most important things you will learn about writing: the first time you write something, it is called a draft. A draft is not a finished piece. It is a starting point.

Think of it like building with clay. First, you make a rough shape. It does not look perfect. Then you smooth it, add details, fix the parts that are wrong. The rough shape is the draft. The smoothing and fixing is called revision.

Every writer in the world makes drafts. Every writer in the world revises. The difference between a good writer and a struggling writer is not talent — it is the willingness to look at what they wrote and say, "I can make this better."

You are going to practice this. Sometimes, after you write something in your notebook, your mentor will say, "Read it out loud to me." When you read it out loud, you will hear places where it does not sound right, or where you forgot something, or where you could say it better. Then you can fix it. That is revision. It is not a punishment. It is what makes writing good.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Daily Notebook Practice [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
The student writes or dictates in their Writer's Notebook every single day. At first, this may be a single sentence and a picture. By the end of the packet, the student should be producing four to eight sentences regularly. The mentor reads the notebook with interest but does not correct it. Conversation about the entries is welcome; red-pen correction is not.

### Study 2: Dictation Sessions [Ongoing for Pre-Writers]
For students who are not yet writing independently, conduct regular dictation sessions. The student composes orally; the mentor transcribes. Then the mentor reads the words back. The student sees their thoughts become visible. Gradually, the student takes over writing individual words, then phrases, then sentences. The transition from dictation to independent writing happens naturally and should not be rushed.

### Study 3: Copywork Practice [2-3 Times Per Week]
Select short passages (two to four sentences) from books the student loves. The student copies them by hand into their copywork journal. After copying, the mentor and student read the passage together. What makes it good? How does it sound? What words does the author use? This is not analysis at an academic level — it is noticing, appreciating, absorbing.

### Study 4: Draw-and-Write [Weekly]
The student draws a scene — real or imagined — and then writes about it. The mentor prompts with questions: "What is happening in your picture? What happened just before this? What does this place smell like? What sounds can you hear?" These questions teach the student to add detail without lecturing about detail.

### Study 5: Story Telling Before Story Writing [As Needed]
When the student seems stuck or resistant, switch to oral mode. "Tell me a story about..." Let them talk. Then say, "Now write down the story you just told me." The oral composition removes the barrier of the blank page.

### Study 6: The First Revision [Late in the Packet]
Choose one piece from the notebook that the student is proud of. Have the student read it aloud. Ask: "Is there anything you want to add? Is there any part that does not sound right?" Make changes together. Then the student writes (or dictates) a "new and improved" version. This is the student's first formal experience of revision. Celebrate it.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Willingness to Write
**Developing well:** The student approaches notebook time without resistance. They may occasionally say "I don't know what to write" but they work through it. They sometimes choose to write outside of scheduled notebook time. They share what they wrote with you voluntarily.

**Developing slowly:** The student resists notebook time. Writing feels like a punishment. They produce the minimum possible and seem relieved when it is over.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the notebook truly unjudged? Have you ever corrected spelling in it? Does the student see you writing? Is the daily session at a time when the student is fresh and alert, or at the end of a long day when everything is hard?

### 2. Oral Composition Fluency
**Developing well:** The student can compose a clear, complete sentence for dictation without excessive prompting. They can tell a short story with a beginning, middle, and end. Their dictated compositions are getting longer and more detailed over weeks.

**Developing slowly:** The student struggles to compose even a single clear sentence. Dictations are fragments or single words. The student cannot sustain a short narrative orally.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you reading aloud to the student daily? (Oral composition is fueled by the language heard in books.) Are you giving the student enough think-time before expecting a response? Some children need five to ten seconds of silence to compose a thought.

### 3. Understanding of "Draft"
**Developing well:** The student uses the word "draft" or the concept naturally. They understand that writing can be changed. When asked to revise, they engage rather than resist. They may spontaneously say "I want to change that part."

**Developing slowly:** The student views writing as permanent and final. Suggestions to change anything feel like criticism. The concept of revision has not taken hold.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you modeled revision yourself? ("I wrote this sentence, but it doesn't sound right. Let me try again.") Have you framed revision positively? The first revision experience should feel like an exciting improvement, not a correction of failure.

### 4. Volume and Fluency Growth
**Developing well:** The student's daily output is growing. What started as one sentence is now three or four. What started as a drawing with a caption is now a drawing with a short paragraph. The student writes with less hesitation than they did at the start of the packet.

**Developing slowly:** Output has not increased over weeks. The student still produces the same minimal amount they produced at the beginning. No growth in sentence length, detail, or complexity.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student getting enough oral language input (read-alouds, conversation, story-telling)? Is the student being asked to write about topics that interest them, or topics assigned by the mentor? At this age, self-chosen topics produce dramatically more output.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Correcting the Notebook
This is the most common and most damaging mistake. The Writer's Notebook is not a place for corrections. It is a safe space for fluency. If you correct it, the student learns to write less (fewer words = fewer errors = less correction). Spelling and grammar instruction happen elsewhere — in copywork, in phonics work, in dictation exercises. The notebook is for free writing.

### Prioritizing Neatness Over Content
If the student is spending more energy on making their letters pretty than on composing thoughts, the priority has inverted. Handwriting practice is separate from writing practice. The notebook may be messy. That is fine. What matters is that it contains the student's thoughts.

### Skipping Dictation for "Real Writing"
Some mentors feel that dictation is not "real" writing and push students to write independently before they are ready. This produces students who associate writing with frustration and struggle. Dictation is real writing. The composition — the thinking, the word choice, the story structure — is the hard part, and the student is doing all of it. The transcription is mechanical. Let the student compose fluently through dictation for as long as they need to.

### Making Copywork Punitive
Copywork should be a calm, pleasant activity — a few minutes of careful, attentive copying from a beloved book. It is not a punishment. It is not "write this sentence ten times." It is the apprenticeship practice of absorbing good prose through the hand. If copywork feels like drudgery, the passages are too long or the books are not engaging enough.

### Neglecting the Read-Aloud
The single most powerful intervention for developing young writers is reading aloud to them — daily, from well-written books. A student who hears beautiful prose absorbs sentence patterns, vocabulary, and rhythms that emerge in their own writing months later. If you are doing nothing else from this packet, read aloud to the student every day.
